paper_map.title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
paper_map.abstract_summary: The mechanical properties and deformation behaviors of a low-carbon low-density Fe35Mn6Al0.1C steel were studied at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). The steel exhibits enhanced strength and ductility at LNT (YS 497 MPa, UTS 945 MPa, TE 75.5%) along with excellent cryogenic impact toughness (254 J). Microstructural analysis reveals that RT deformation is dominated by dislocation substructure refinement with late twinning, whereas LNT deformation involves early and extensive deformation twinning alongside dislocation refinement, leading to a four-stage strain hardening behavior.
paper_map.research_objective: To reveal the microstructure evolution and its effects on mechanical properties of a low-carbon low-density austenitic steel at both ambient and cryogenic temperatures, with particular focus on the deformation twinning mechanism under high stacking fault energy.
material_systems: austenite-based duplex steel with a small amount of ferrite (~3%)
κ-carbide (mentioned as a potential precipitate in high-carbon variants)
main_process_variables: hot rolling to 16.5 mm diameter rods
solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h followed by oil quenching
tensile testing at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT)
Charpy V-notch impact testing at RT and LNT
key_sections: Introduction: reviews low-density steels, highlights the knowledge gap on low-carbon variants, and states the research objectives
Materials and experimental design: describes alloy preparation, processing, and characterization techniques
Results: includes initial microstructures, mechanical properties, EBSD and TEM analysis of tensile microstructural evolution, and deformation structure of impact specimens
Discussion: evaluates the steel as a cryogenic candidate, analyzes the deformation twinning mechanism under high SFE, and discusses multistage hardening and flow stress contributions
Conclusion: summarizes the main findings regarding deformation mechanisms at RT and LNT
